topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box sat on the kitchen counter, a monument to my mother's bizarre organizational system. Inside, neatly stacked, were every single piece of paper from my entire academic career. Calculus proofs, Shakespearean essays, even that disastrous history project from seventh grade. I stared at the sheer volume, the relentless accumulation of forgotten effort. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat. My hands clenched into fists so tight my knuckles turned white. "What in the world are you doing?" I finally managed, the words sharp and brittle. The air in the room felt thick, charged with a furious energy that made my chest tighten. I wanted to sweep the box off the counter and watch it shatter.

[story 2]

I found the collection tucked away in the attic, behind a stack of moth-eaten blankets. When I pulled out the first binder, a tremor ran through my hands. It wasn't just the papers; it was the deliberate, suffocating nature of it. Every assignment, every graded test, cataloged with meticulous care. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I slammed the binder shut, the sound echoing loudly in the dusty silence. "This is absurd," I hissed, my voice shaking with barely contained fury. I paced the small space, my boots thudding against the floorboards. The injustice of it all felt like a physical weight pressing down on my diaphragm, demanding release.

[story 3]

It was the sheer, obsessive detail that broke me. Every doodle, every margin note, every hastily scribbled answer was preserved. I picked up a worksheet from sophomore year, my fingers trembling as I traced the ink. A deep, visceral churning started in my stomach, a feeling of absolute betrayal washing over me. I dropped the paper onto the floor with a violent slap. "How could you keep all of this?" My voice was dangerously quiet, a low vibration that promised serious repercussions. I stood there, breathing heavily, my jaw locked so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I couldn't look at her; the sight of the organized chaos was too much to bear.
